_meta.description: Waiting-room jokes for long process wait loops. Displayed at 90s+ intervals.
_meta.style: Awkwardly funny IT-admin humour: slow apps, old RAM DIMMs, legacy systems.

jokes: Why did the app take 4 minutes to load? Each RAM stick needed written consent individually.
Your DDR3 DIMMs are so old they remember when 'The Cloud' was just a weather forecast.
Windows Sandbox is warming up. Your RAM is also warming up -- it briefly confused itself with a biscuit tin.
Loading... Meanwhile, this machine's memory modules are filing their annual leave requests.
Fun fact: your RAM sticks have grandchildren who work at AWS. They send postcards. The originals are too slow to read them.
Still waiting. The sandbox asked the old DIMMs a question and is politely waiting for them to finish thinking.
Your PC is computing. Your RAM is doing its best. Your RAM's best is... colourful.
The process would be faster but two of your RAM slots are currently in a passive-aggressive standoff about which one owns address 0x7FFE.
If your memory modules were any slower, archaeologists would need to document them before boot.
Loading takes time. Good things come to those who wait. Your RAM has been waiting since the Obama administration.
Did you know? Your DIMM sticks communicate via a bus. The bus runs on a schedule from 2009. It is almost never on time.
Initialising sandbox... Your RAM raised its hand to ask a question. We are still waiting for it to stand up.
Your app is large. Remarkably, heroically, impressively large. Like a 400-thread Java monolith wearing a trenchcoat labelled 'microservice'.
Still here. Just so you know, the garbage collector ran 47 times and has not collected anything useful. Relatable.
The simulation is starting. Please hold. Your memory bus is currently being rerouted through the memory of a 2007 era Inspiron.
Your RAM DIMMs are so venerable they have a Wikipedia page under 'Historical Computing Artifacts (Pre-SSD Era)'.
Status: Waiting. The sandbox is politely queuing. The old DIMMs are treating this queue like a suggestion.
Loading screen protip: if you tilt your head and stare at this message long enough, it still does nothing. But it shows character.
Your PC just paged 2GB to disk. The disk responded by asking for a moment to locate its reading glasses.
Your RAM has seen things. Terrible things. Mostly legacy VBScript. It has never fully recovered.
The sandbox process is alive but thoughtful. Much like your memory modules, which are still deciding whether to participate.
Your system memory thinks multitasking means doing one thing slowly while making several other things wait politely.
Why is it slow? Because 32-bit applications are like trying to move furniture using only Post-it notes as leverage.
Your DDR3 is DDR-three-times-the-time.
Warming up the environment. Your RAM sticks are stretching. This is taking longer than anticipated because they are not very flexible.
You know you have old RAM when it shows up to work but sends its thoughts ahead by fax.
The app spawned 14 child processes and three of them are now asking the operating system for emotional validation.
Your memory reads at 800MHz. Your patience runs at 0Hz. Somebody in this arrangement is underperforming.
At current allocation speed, the sandbox will be ready approximately 6 millennia after the heat death of the universe. Progress.
Your RAM is computing. Or meditating. The difference is largely academic at this throughput.
The sandbox is initialising. Your old DIMMs are handling it like a government form: one field at a time, with pauses for deliberation.
Fun fact: If you printed your app's heap usage, the paper stack would be taller than your monitor stand. Which, granted, is not very tall.
Still loading. Your RAM just sent the CPU a 'read receipt'. The CPU is still composing its reply in its head.
Your PC's memory is doing its best. Its best is the computational equivalent of trying to explain the internet to a tortoise.
Progress update: The sandbox is loading. Your RAM sticks are debating which one of them is the primary. The debate is tied, as usual.
This wait is the software equivalent of watching a glacier make a left turn.
Your system has 16GB of RAM and is currently using all of it to hold a Chrome tab open from last Tuesday.
Loading... No really, loading. Please don't leave. The old DIMMs get anxious when unobserved.
Your application is thinking very hard. So hard that the memory controller took a quick nap and woke up refreshed but no faster.
Fun update: three modules of your RAM are working. One is 'resting its eyes'. We don't mention which one.
Patience required. Your RAM takes 'eventual consistency' very literally.
The sandbox is coming up, same as how an old freight elevator comes up: loudly, slowly, with great creaking, and probably fine.
Your RAM hasn't been this busy since it tried to run Internet Explorer 8 and Windows Defender at the same time on a Tuesday.
Waiting... Your memory bus just hit peak traffic. Rush hour on the DDR3 expressway: all lanes doing 20MPH for no visible reason.
Still computing. Your application is so large it has its own gravitational field and is currently attracting minor DLLs from nearby processes.
Status check: Everything is technically working. Just slowly. Like a cloud upload on a rain-soaked 4G connection next to a concrete wall.
Your RAM just paged out three background services to reclaim 48MB. It was incredibly proud of itself. Give it this. It earned it.
Loading sequence complete... just kidding. But keep watching. Something will definitely happen eventually. Almost certainly.
